text: The surgical team has docked the robotic system on the right-hand side. The surgeon begins dissection on the left lower quadrant, near the area where the midline was previously crossed.

The surgeon dissects the left retro-rectus space, carefully maintaining the epigastric vessels against the rectus muscle while separating the posterior sheath from the edge of the left side of the hernia defect.
The surgeon observes a significant gap in the posterior sheath, with a possible attenuation of the semilunar line, which does not appear to be a full disruption.
The video shows the area where the previous surgeon initiated the left-sided TAR (Transversus Abdominis Release) flap.
The surgeon considers removing the mesh from the rectus muscle to preserve the posterior structure for closure.
The surgeon carefully removes the mesh from the rectus muscle, focusing on preserving the underlying structures.
The surgeon encounters heavily scarred tissue during the dissection of the left retro-rectus space, affecting the separation of anatomical structures.
The surgeon carefully navigates along the medial edge of the rectus muscle, maintaining a position behind the rectus to reach the opposite side of the gap.
The surgeon identifies a small area of free space, facilitating easier dissection around the rectus muscle.
The surgeon encounters the peritoneum protruding into the abdominal cavity from the superior part of the hernia, obstructing the surgical field.

The surgeon observes a full disruption in the middle of the Progrit mesh, with a small bridge of mesh remaining intact. The decision is made to remove the bridge to create a single, common hernia defect.
The surgeon excises a portion of tissue from the hernia defect area and prepares it for further processing.
The surgeon attempts to locate the medial edge of the rectus muscle, facing difficulty in identifying the precise anatomical boundary.
The surgeon attempts to access the retro-rectus space through the hernia sac and scar plate, working to detach the mesh from the rectus muscle. The area is heavily scarred, as expected.
The surgeon carefully navigates the surgical plane on one side, avoiding creating holes or damaging the rectus muscle while dissecting within the retro-rectus space.
The surgeon develops the space of Retzius in the midline of the hypogastrium, carefully maintaining the epigastric vessels against the rectus muscle.
The surgeon navigates towards the pubic tubercle, avoiding going inferior as the hernia defect is located above the umbilicus.

The surgeon identifies the arcuate line on the left-hand side and begins a bottoms-up transversus abdominis release (TAR) to define the anatomy near the epigastrium, aiming to maintain the correct surgical plane.
The surgeon initiates a bottoms-up transversus abdominis release (TAR) on the left side, carefully working within the identified plane to avoid damaging the rectus muscle.
The surgeon works laterally before returning to a medial approach, manipulating tissue in the surgical field.
The surgeon observes a neurovascular bundle descending within a scarred plane.
The surgeon identifies a neurovascular bundle within a scarred plane and carefully dissects in front of it, maintaining a medial position relative to the bundle.

The surgeon identifies the superior edge of the hernia defect and attempts to re-enter the proper surgical plane, cautiously navigating to avoid inadvertently starting the transversus abdominis release (TAR) on this side.
The surgeon cuts through the scar plate, observing muscle contractions indicative of cutting the transversus abdominis muscle, while confirming the proper plane laterally.

The surgeon continues the dissection, moving slightly medial into the hernia defect, staying close to the posterior aspect of the rectus muscle to gain more space for identifying the transversus abdominis muscle, which is expected to be further lateral.

The surgeon activates a surgical tool to assist in navigating the heavily scarred tissue. They create multiple openings while preserving enough of the hernia sac from the center of the defect to facilitate closure.

The surgeon continues the dissection, creating additional openings in the scarred plane between the rectus muscle and the mesh. The mesh is visible at the lateral edge, with the rectus muscle superiorly towards the top of the screen.
The surgeon carefully dissects the peritoneum from the rectus muscle, revealing a gap in the posterior sheath where flap closure had previously separated.

The surgeon carefully dissects along the back of the rectus muscle, moving laterally to the mesh. The semilunar line is visible, and the surgeon connects the dissection plane from the inferior TAR to the current plane.
The surgeon inspects the area with visible scarring, attributed to the previous surgery, where the peritoneal flap and posterior sheath flap were sutured closed.
The surgeon continues the transversus abdominis release, working through the scarred plane where some muscle fibers are visible within the scar tissue.
The surgeon continues the dissection along the semilunar line, frequently checking the progress and ensuring the dissection plane is maintained.

The surgeon carefully dissects along the back of the rectus muscle, navigating through scar tissue on the lateral portion near the program mesh. The transversus abdominis muscle is identified, and dissection continues in the proper plane against the bare muscle. The area is less scarred, allowing the surgeon to work back medially, removing posterior elements such as peritoneum and scar plate.

The surgeon observes the transversus abdominis muscle positioned in the middle of a posterior gap, noting the absence of a true defect in the semilunar line or a lateral hernia, although the line appears slightly wider than usual.

The surgeon carefully navigates the surgical field, maintaining proximity to the transversus abdominis muscle visible above, while the peritoneum and pre-transverse salis fascia are seen at the bottom of the screen.

The surgeon continues lateral dissection and then moves back medially, having developed a space above and below the transversus abdominis muscle. The tissue in this area separates easily, indicating less scarring.

The surgeon dissects widely and high above the costal margin, focusing on the superior edge of the defect. The dissection proceeds subxiphoid, connecting previous dissections from the right to the left side, with the diaphragm visible in the upper part of the screen.
The surgeon continues to dissect widely, approaching the diaphragm area. The peritoneum is being carefully pulled down, with visible stickiness in the tissue, while ensuring not to push too far.

The surgeon completes the dissection by extending it inferiorly to the full length, ensuring a wide area for mesh overlap. The costal margin is visible, and the dissection reaches its superior extent, showing the completed area.
